content.full_text:
A-73-1499

January 6, 1978


Patricia Ostler
City Clerk
City of Mill Valley
P.O. Box 31
Mill Valley, California 94991

Dear Pat:

Thank you very much for your letter of December 22 concerning
the $50 reporting threshold. Your letter has been distributed
to several staff members who are involved in drafting legislation concerning raising the threshold and I am sure they are
very appreciative of your comments.

Thanks again for your interest.

Very truly yours,


Regina Siciliano-Kutchins
Technical Assistance Program
Coordinator


RSK:bw

CITY OF MILL VALLEY                                California
P.O. BOX 31          415-383-6175                           94941

December 22, 1977


Regina Kutchins
Fair Political Practices Commission
P O Box 807
Sacramento CA 95814

Dear Gina:

Your latest newsletter contained a reference to the
recently completed Political Reform Act Study and
to a recommendation that the $50 reporting threshold
be raised.

For a city our size, 13,250 population, this would
create a reporting form on campaign income and
expenditures that would be meaningless. The contributions
in our town seem to be $49.50 and less. This is a
direct result of the current regulations of reporting
$50 or larger.

If you feel there is a need to raise the reporting
figure, please consider the possibility of increasing
it only for those groups of reporting individuals who
handle the larger sums of money.

I would like to see our campaign reporting minimum at
$1.00, the way it used to be. We have not had a good
reading of candidates' support since the Political
Reform Act was adopted.

Sincerely,

Patricia L. Ostler
City Clerk

sections.question_synthetic: Should the $50 campaign reporting threshold under the Political Reform Act be raised, and if so, should different thresholds apply to different groups of reporting individuals?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC acknowledges the city clerk's concerns about the $50 reporting threshold and confirms that her comments have been distributed to staff members involved in drafting legislation to raise the threshold.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document lacks formal Q&A section headers typical of FPPC advice letters. This appears to be incoming correspondence (December 22, 1977 letter from Ostler) followed by FPPC's brief acknowledgment response (January 5, 1978). The response does not provide formal legal guidance or conclusion, only acknowledges receipt and distribution of comments. Confidence reduced due to informal format and lack of typical advice letter structure.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Should the $50 campaign reporting threshold under the Political Reform Act be raised, and if so, should different thresholds apply to different groups of reporting individuals?

CONCLUSION: The FPPC acknowledges the city clerk's concerns about the $50 reporting threshold and confirms that her comments have been distributed to staff members involved in drafting legislation to raise the threshold.

BOX 31 415-383-6175 94941 December 22, 1977 Regina Kutchins Fair Political Practices Commission P O Box 807 Sacramento CA 95814 Dear Gina: Your latest newsletter contained a reference to the recently completed Political Reform Act Study and to a recommendation that the $50 reporting threshold be raised. embedding.summary: This document consists of correspondence between Patricia Ostler, City Clerk of Mill Valley, and Regina Kutchins of the FPPC regarding concerns about the $50 campaign reporting threshold established by the Political Reform Act. Ostler argues that for a city of 13,250 people, the current threshold creates meaningless reporting forms and requests consideration of either raising the threshold or applying different thresholds to different groups of reporting individuals.
